# Research Report: UI patterns for sensemaking of AI-generated text

**Generated by**: deep-research-arxiv loop
**Research depth**: 3 iterations, 24 queries issued

## Executive Summary

The arxiv literature on UI patterns for sensemaking of AI-generated text is rich across five intersecting facets. On **sensemaking frameworks**, the field has moved beyond importing Weick and Pirolli-Card into HCI and is now generating bespoke cognitive models for LLM interaction — notably SCISENSE's eight-stage operationalization of scientific ideation [4] and premise-governance models that shift trust from conversational fluency to auditable reasoning [5]. Visual workspaces as intermediate representations between human cognition and LLM generation are a recurring design pattern [1][2].

On **explainable attribution**, the literature is mature and fragmenting along granularity lines: token-level (TokenShapley [30]), sentence-level (Thought Anchors [29], SAFE [9]), and plan-level (Attributable Plan Generation [6]). Two comprehensive surveys [31][32] now codify the space. The key UI-relevant finding is that attribution granularity directly shapes verification behavior — sentence-level attribution reduces fact-checking, while token-level increases AI agreement [14]. For **interactive refinement**, the tension between structured and freeform editing is emerging as a central design axis. Structured approaches (OOPrompt [34], IterGen [35], R³ [12]) accelerate planning and focused revision but add latency and disrupt chat continuity; freeform approaches preserve exploratory flow but struggle with targeted edits [13][42].

On **uncertainty communication**, the evidence now strongly favors granular displays. Token-level entropy visualization (LLMbench [53], LogitScope [54]) outperforms output-level confidence scores in calibrating user trust [14]. However, MetaFaith [16] shows LLMs are natively poor at faithful confidence expression (61% improvement with metacognitive prompting is achievable but not yet deployed in UIs), and selective abstention interfaces carry equity risks [52]. On **comparative sensemaking**, interleaved, semantically-aligned displays (Gero et al.'s PDC algorithm [21], LLMartini [22]) consistently outperform linear side-by-side lists for remixing, outlier detection, and difference identification. Debate-style contrastive prompting [25] and pairwise ELO ranking [27] provide structured comparison primitives that could be surfaced in UIs.

Two cross-cutting gaps persist: (1) no controlled ablation studies isolate side-by-side vs. interleaved layout as a single design variable; and (2) no unified interface combines attribution provenance, interactive refinement, and comparative evaluation in a single tool — each exists separately but integration remains unexplored. The literature is strongest on single-session lab studies (N=18–200) and weakest on longitudinal field deployments with professional users.

## Key Findings

### Sensemaking Frameworks for Machine-Generated Text

The theoretical foundations for LLM sensemaking have evolved significantly since 2023. Early work adapted general sensemaking theory: Selenite [2] repurposed LLMs as knowledge retrievers to auto-generate comprehensive overviews, addressing the cold-start problem where users cannot formulate questions in unfamiliar domains. Three studies confirmed that LLM-generated overviews accelerated information processing and improved comprehension, with the system adapting as users gained domain familiarity.

More recent work builds bespoke cognitive models. SCISENSE [4] operationalizes scientific ideation as eight sequential cognitive stages derived from Pirolli & Card (2005), creating a trajectory dataset and training models (3B–70B) that reproduce known research pathways. Target-trained models produced 2.0% better trajectory quality and more novel outputs than inference-trained baselines, with downstream coding agents producing higher-quality artifacts — suggesting that constraining sensemaking to structured cognitive stages improves creative output, not reduces it.

The most theoretically ambitious framework is Jain's premise governance model [5], which reframes human-AI sensemaking from answer-generation to collaborative premise governance for deep-uncertainty decisions. Drawing on Weick (1995) and Klein et al. (2006), it proposes a discrepancy-driven control loop with three typed discrepancies (teleological, epistemic, procedural) and commitment gating. Trust attaches to auditable premises rather than conversational fluency — a direct design implication for UI architecture.

Visual workspaces as intermediate representations are a recurring motif. Tang et al. [1] showed that human-generated schematic visual workspaces, when injected into GPT-4 prompts, steer summarization toward more accurate outputs aligned with ground truth. Polymind [38] extends this to prewriting with multiple LLM agents and visual diagramming, finding users anthropomorphize the LLM as a collaborator and iterate more rapidly. VIS-ReAct [39] demonstrates a two-agent architecture where an analysis agent interprets semantic interactions in visual workspaces to infer user intentions, while a refinement agent updates reports — preserving semantic fidelity through transparent inference.

Cognitive processing of machine-generated text reveals a paradox: LLM output fluency creates a metacognitive cue that users mistake for competence [37] — the "LLM Fallacy." Temperature-scaled surprisal (~2.5) better fits human reading times than raw LLM probabilities (up to 89% improvement in delta log-likelihood), suggesting LLM probability distributions are too "sharp" relative to human cognitive load [35]. Fine-tuning LMs to predict reading times improves psychometric fit but degrades language modeling quality — better cognitive models are worse LMs [36].

### Explainable Text Generation and Attribution

Attribution has evolved from coarse post-hoc methods to fine-grained, in-generation approaches spanning multiple granularities. At the **token level**, TokenShapley [30] combines Shapley value-based data attribution with KNN retrieval, achieving 11–23% improvement over prior token-level methods — specifically addressing the failure of sentence-level methods for keywords like numbers, years, and names. ContextCite [7] uses linear surrogate models for contributive attribution, enabling three downstream applications: statement verification, response improvement via context pruning, and poisoning attack detection.

At the **sentence level**, Thought Anchors [29] provides three complementary methods (black-box counterfactual resampling, white-box attention aggregation, causal attention suppression) to identify "thought anchors" — planning and uncertainty-management sentences with outsized impact on final output. The causal structure of reasoning traces predicts problem difficulty and reveals whether reasoning is sequential or diffuse. SAFE [9] achieves 95% accuracy in predicting needed reference counts per sentence and generalizes to documents spanning hundreds to thousands of sentences.

At the **plan level**, Fierro et al. [6] demonstrate that question-sequence plans serving as content blueprints consistently improve attribution quality over LLM pipelines without planning. Blueprint-model citations are more accurate than direct generation. SymGen [8] interleaves symbolic references to structured data fields within generated text, enabling fine-grained provenance without specialized architectures — a 20% reduction in human verification time while maintaining fluency.

Two comprehensive surveys now codify the space. Pang et al. [31] propose a dual-paradigm taxonomy (prior-based proactive traceability embedding vs. posterior-based retrospective inference) across four dimensions (model, model structure, training data, external data). Li et al. [32] cover direct model-driven, post-retrieval, and post-generation attribution, with a maintained GitHub repository tracking the field.

For faithfulness evaluation, LLM-as-a-judge metrics show the highest correlation with human judgment [41], and QA-based metrics like FEQA [42] significantly outperform word-overlap, embedding, and learned LM metrics, particularly for highly abstractive summaries. Post-hoc interpretability methods (gradients, LIME) capture more useful insights than attention weights alone [43], and gradient-based token attribution (Jacobian Scopes [44]) reveals political biases and in-context learning mechanisms.

### Interactive Review and Refinement of LLM Output

The central design tension in interactive refinement is **structured vs. freeform editing**. OOPrompt [34] directly addresses this: transforming linear prompts into structured, manipulable artifacts makes planning and iterative writing faster and clearer, but adds latency and disrupts chat continuity. Formative study (N=20) with a follow-up prototype validation confirmed both benefits and costs. Freeform approaches excel at ambiguous, exploratory tasks but struggle with targeted revision.

This tension manifests across multiple systems. AnchoredAI [11] anchors AI feedback to specific text spans via an Anchoring Context Window and update-aware retrieval, producing more targeted revisions and better user ownership than chat-based interfaces. Textfocals [10] takes the opposite approach — sidebar-based summaries, questions, and advice without direct text generation — preserving user authorship but revealing challenges in document navigation and prompt engineering. The R³ system [12] formalizes an iterative accept/reject loop with edit intention classification (fluency, coherence, clarity, style), finding that human-machine interaction produces higher-quality revisions with fewer iterations than pure human or pure LLM revision.

For **cognitive effort measurement**, the field is moving beyond output-only metrics. MindCopilot [46] formalizes co-writing as a Human-in-the-Loop MDP with Hierarchical Acceptance Rate and Knowledge-aware Editing Distance, evaluated across 16 domains (1,688 queries + N=30 user study). Process-aware feedback grounded in keystroke logs and revision snapshots improves structural evaluation and process-sensitive justification compared to product-only feedback [45]. Compression-based edit distance achieves Pearson correlations up to 0.87 with actual keystrokes, substantially outperforming BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore. ScholaWrite [48] provides the first end-to-end scholarly writing dataset with keystroke-level recording over multiple months (5 CS preprints, ~62K text changes, 15 cognitive writing intentions), revealing gaps between human writing micro-dynamics and current LLM assistance.

Structured generation infrastructure enables refinement patterns not possible with freeform interfaces. IterGen [35] supports forward and backward movement through LLM output via grammar symbols as navigational abstractions and KV cache management — applied to reducing privacy leakage and improving SQL/Vega-Lite query accuracy. Interaction-Required Suggestions [13] take a deliberately efficiency-sacrificing approach: predictive-text interaction requiring users to type assistant responses word-by-word, and opportunity highlighting showing potential edit points. Both prioritize agency and cognitive engagement over throughput.

### Uncertainty Communication in Language Model Output

The granularity at which uncertainty is displayed is a critical design dimension with measurable behavioral effects. Villavicencio et al. [14] conducted a large-scale study (N=192) comparing output-level, relation-level, and token-level uncertainty communication. Token-level uncertainty increased user agreement with AI; relation-level uncertainty *reduced* external verification behaviors (fact-checking). This pattern — that fine-grained uncertainty makes users trust more while coarse-grained uncertainty makes them verify less — has direct implications for UI design: granularity is not just a fidelity choice but a behavioral intervention.

For **display techniques**, LLMbench [53] provides the most comprehensive tool: four analytical overlays (Probabilities, Differences, Tone, Structure) with five modes including heatmaps, entropy sparklines, pixel maps, and 3D probability terrains showing the "counterfactual history" of each word. LogitScope [54] offers model-agnostic token-level entropy and varentropy analysis. LMdiff [26] provides token-by-token probability distribution comparison between two models using a blue-red diverging color scheme. At the mechanistic level, "entropy neurons" in LayerNorm null spaces modulate output distribution entropy across GPT-2, Pythia, Phi-2, and LLaMA2 [55], while small linear probes on frozen embeddings can separate epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty at the token level [56].

On **calibration**, the evidence is sobering. MetaFaith [16] — the first systematic study of faithful confidence calibration — finds LLMs "largely fail" at aligning linguistic uncertainty expressions with intrinsic uncertainty. Its metacognitive prompting approach achieves 61% improvement in faithfulness and 83% win rate in human judgment, but this is not yet deployed in production UIs. Steyvers et al. [15] showed that longer explanations increase user confidence even without accuracy improvement, but calibrating explanations to reflect internal model confidence narrows both calibration and discrimination gaps. Yang et al. [17] benchmark verbalized confidence across 10 datasets, 11 LLMs, and 17 prompt methods, finding reliability strongly depends on *how* the model is asked — well-calibrated scores are achievable with specific prompt methods.

**Selective prediction and abstention** interfaces introduce a new design challenge. HCMA [49] routes queries along an LLM hierarchy using model-intrinsic uncertainty, achieving 30% error reduction on MMLU at 20% abstention rate with only 50–100 labeled calibration examples. PVD [50] uses interactive proof theory (prover defends, verifier accepts/challenges/rejects) producing a ~30pp gap on GPQA Diamond. However, Unequal Uncertainty [52] warns that abstention based on uncertainty can inadvertently exacerbate disparities affecting under-represented groups — selective friction (requiring deliberate user overrides) preserves transparency and encourages cautious judgment without differential impact.

### Comparative Sensemaking of Multi-Output Text

The strongest finding is that **interleaved, semantically-aligned displays outperform linear side-by-side lists**. Gero et al. [21] introduced Positional Diction Clustering (PDC), an algorithm that identifies sentences with similar content and position across multiple LLM responses, enabling an interleaved layout that groups analogous parts with redundancies grayed out. A controlled study (n=24) plus 8 case studies found the interleaved layout particularly useful for remixing response parts and identifying rare/outlier components. Participants found significantly more differences between models than with a baseline linear list. LLMartini [22] extends this paradigm by decomposing responses into semantically aligned segments based on task-specific criteria, merging consensus content and highlighting differences via color coding. A user study (N=18) showed significant improvements in completion time, cognitive load, and satisfaction over conventional manual comparison — 93% of users reported comparing outputs across models.

**Structured comparison frameworks** provide reusable primitives. Q-STRUM Debate [25] uses two-agent debate-style prompting (Alice and Bob arguing pros and cons) to produce contrastive summaries, achieving 80%+ win rates for contrast and diversity on subjective datasets. The Product of Experts framework [27] treats pairwise comparisons as Gaussian experts, yielding closed-form solutions for optimal ranking using as few as 2% of all possible comparisons — directly informing confidence-aware ranking displays. PAIRS [24] reframes evaluation as a ranking problem with uncertainty-guided search-based rank aggregation, achieving state-of-the-art on long-form generation evaluation. LLM Comparator [28] iteratively designed with researchers and engineers, helps users understand when and why one model outperforms another through structured side-by-side qualitative analysis.

For **within-model vs. cross-model comparison**, LLMartini [22] provides a dual-mode interface: visual comparison for divergent responses plus fusion for similar ones. ALIGN [61] enables prompt-based attribute alignment for side-by-side comparison along fine-grained dimensions (fairness, risk aversion, moral desert), demonstrated on public opinion surveys and medical triage. DreamSheets [65] provides a two-week longitudinal study comparing expert artists (n=5) with amateurs (n=12), finding both groups build mental models of input-output mapping but experts more strategically delegate foraging to AI while retaining control over synthesis.

**Evaluation methodology** for comparison interfaces is maturing. Rating Roulette [57] exposes low intra-rater reliability in LLM judges (Krippendorff's Alpha: Llama 3.1 = 0.33, Qwen-3 = 0.79), questioning the common practice of using a single judge. SEAM [59] provides the first benchmark specifically for multi-document tasks with stochastic evaluation resampling. A significant primacy effect — first-seen document weighted more heavily — was documented in multi-document summarization [61], with direct implications for how response order is presented in comparison UIs. The ELOQUENT 2025 shared task [63] found that evaluators erroneously rate garbled answers as acceptable, indicating that comparison interfaces need built-in quality sanity checks.

### Cross-Cutting Themes

**Attribution and refinement integration**: Attribution Gradients [33] consolidates evidence amounts, supporting/contradictory excerpts, source links, and contextual explanation into a single location with drill-down capability. Lab studies showed increased depth of source understanding versus standard citation and document QA designs. This is the closest existing work to a unified attribution+refinement interface, but falls short of also integrating comparative evaluation.

**Longitudinal and expert-novice evidence**: The field relies heavily on single-session lab studies. Exceptions include: DreamSheets [65] (two-week longitudinal), Augmenting Expert Cognition [66] (expert strategic delegation of foraging to AI while retaining synthesis control, 16% faster sensemaking with GenAI), and Verification Burden [67] (multi-disciplinary study identifying three gaps: lack of trust, persistent verification burden, and fragmented multi-tool pipelines — proposing six design goals including citation grounding and verification at every step).

## Coverage Gaps

1. **Controlled ablation of comparison layouts**: No published study systematically ablates side-by-side vs. interleaved display for multi-output text comparison while controlling for other design variables. Gero et al. [21] and LLMartini [22] provide the closest evidence but compare system-level interfaces, not isolated layout dimensions.

2. **Unified attribution + refinement + comparison interface**: Individual tools address each dimension, but no work proposes or evaluates a single interface combining attribution provenance, interactive refinement, and comparative evaluation. Attribution Gradients [33] and LLMartini [22] each span two of three dimensions.

3. **Longitudinal field studies**: Only two studies exceed two-week deployment durations (DreamSheets [65], ScholaWrite [48]). Expert-novice differences in LLM output sensemaking strategies are documented [66] but not yet translated into adaptive UI designs.

4. **Beyond-text modalities**: The literature focuses overwhelmingly on text. Sensemaking of structured outputs (tables, code, structured data), multi-modal outputs (text+image generation), and interactive data visualizations generated by LLMs are under-explored.

5. **Component-level vs. whole-document refinement**: While structured editing (OOPrompt [34]) and chunk-based approval are discussed theoretically, no dedicated arxiv paper directly compares component-level vs. whole-document refinement architectures for LLM review.

6. **PIAC / Construction-Integration theory**: These specific cognitive models do not appear as named frameworks in contemporary arxiv papers on machine-generated text comprehension, though their measurement paradigms (reading time, eye-tracking) are actively studied [35][36].

7. **Evaluation benchmarks for comparison interfaces**: Metrics for comparison interface quality (comparative accuracy, preference consistency, inter-rater reliability) exist for LLM judges [57][59] but have not been adapted into standardized benchmarks for UI evaluation of comparison tools.

## Conclusion

The arxiv literature on UI patterns for sensemaking of AI-generated text is converging on several high-confidence findings: interleaved, semantically-aligned displays outperform linear lists for multi-output comparison [21][22]; granular uncertainty displays (token-level) produce different verification behaviors than coarse displays (output-level) [14]; structured editing accelerates targeted revision while freeform editing preserves exploratory flow [34]; and plan-based or symbolic attribution provides more verifiable provenance than post-hoc explanation [6][8]. These findings are supported by controlled user studies (N=18–200) and are grounded in cognitive theory [4][5].

Lower-confidence but promising directions include debate-style contrastive prompting surfaced in UIs [25], metacognitive prompting for calibrated confidence expression [16], hierarchical abstention with calibrated thresholds [50], and process-aware feedback grounded in keystroke logs rather than output text [46][47]. The field would benefit from: (1) controlled ablation studies isolating comparison layout as a single variable, (2) longitudinal deployments exceeding two weeks, (3) unified interfaces spanning attribution, refinement, and comparison in a single tool, and (4) standardized benchmarks for evaluating comparison interface quality that account for documented LLM judge reliability problems [58] and primacy effects [61]. Overall confidence in the synthesized findings is **moderate-high**: the evidence base is broad (70 unique arxiv papers) and recent (median year 2025), but is dominated by single-session lab studies and lacks the longitudinal, in-situ deployment data that would elevate confidence to high.
